title: Spinning a three-ply yarn
subtitle: How to spin three matched singles and ply them simultaneously for a round, dense yarn
excerpt: A three-ply yarn is formed by plying three singles together simultaneously rather than two. The result is rounder in cross-section, more dense, and produces less knitting bias than a two-ply equivalent. The challenge lies in spinning three sufficiently consistent singles and then managing three bobbins at once during plying.
sourceNotes: Three-ply construction is a standard spinning guild technique. Guidance on consistent singles and ply-twist balance is observation-led from guild teaching and UK spinning publications.

glossaryTerms.definition: A yarn structure formed by plying three singles together simultaneously in the direction opposite to their individual twist. Three-ply yarn has a rounder, denser cross-section than two-ply yarn of the same weight and produces less bias in balanced knitting patterns.

Twist inserted during plying, which runs in the opposite direction to the singles twist. Ply twist binds the singles together into a stable yarn and partially cancels the energy accumulated in the singles. The correct level of ply twist produces a balanced yarn that hangs straight without coiling.

A finished yarn in which the singles twist and ply twist are in equilibrium. When held at its midpoint with both ends free, a balanced yarn hangs straight without looping or spiralling in either direction.

content.text: yarn is rounder and denser than a two-ply at the same weight. It suits hard-wearing socks, sturdy mittens, and woven projects where a smooth, round structure is needed. The technique builds on two-ply skills. Spin a consistent single reliably before attempting three-ply; any inconsistency in one of the three singles will dominate the finished yarn.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair, scarves, and cuffs away from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast and catches fabric instantly.

items.name: Fibre for spinning: combed top or carded roving, 150 g
Spinning wheel with at least three bobbins
Lazy kate or three-bobbin holder to hold singles during plying
Measuring tape for wraps per inch

content.text: Divide your fibre supply by weight into three equal portions before spinning. Spin each portion from the same point in the preparation to the same density and twist level, winding onto a separate bobbin. Weigh each bobbin after spinning: if one bobbin is significantly lighter than the others, the singles are inconsistent. Check wraps per inch on each bobbin by winding a sample around a ruler; all three should give the same count within one wrap per 2.5 cm. Matched singles produce a round, balanced three-ply; mismatched singles produce a lopsided yarn dominated by the thinnest single.
Step 2: Set up for three-bobbin plying

Place all three bobbins on a lazy kate or three-bobbin holder with light, even tension on each bobbin. Thread each single separately through your fingers so all three are held distinct and cannot cross before they enter the plying zone. A simple arrangement is to hold all three between the first and second fingers of the back hand, keeping them separated. If they cross upstream of your fingers, the three-ply will have uneven sections where two strands twist together and one sits loose.
Step 3: Ply all three singles together
Ply in the direction opposite to your singles twist, as for a two-ply. The

required for a three-ply is slightly less than for a two-ply of the same singles: because the three strands pack against each other more tightly, the structure locks more quickly. A ratio one to two points lower than your singles ratio is a reliable starting point. Test the balance by letting a length of the finished three-ply hang freely from a central point. A
will hang with no tendency to loop or coil. If the yarn spirals, add more ply twist; if it still has lively singles energy, reduce the ply twist and re-test.

Wind the three-ply yarn onto the niddy-noddy into a skein, tie at four points, and soak in warm water for 15 minutes. Snap once to set the twist and hang to dry with a small weight. Three-ply blooms noticeably on washing as the three strands press against each other and round out; the finished skein will be slightly shorter and wider than the same length of two-ply from the same fibre.

items.problem: The three-ply has a pronounced spiral in one direction after finishing
items.cause: The ply twist was not enough to balance the singles energy
items.fix: Unwind a length and re-ply with more twist in the plying direction. Three-ply requires less ply twist than two-ply but still needs enough to fully counter the singles twist.

items.problem: One strand appears to dominate and sit on the outside of the three-ply
items.cause: The three singles were not evenly tensioned on the lazy kate, allowing one to feed faster than the others
items.fix: Increase the brake tension on the dominant bobbin so it feeds at the same rate as the others. Check that all three singles remain separated at the finger guide point before entering the plying zone.
